label: Aristotelian cosmology

scope.school: Aristotle's natural philosophy (Physics, De Caelo, Metaphysics 12, De Anima)
limitations: Draft awaiting human domain review (D2-001); review needed before any scholarly authority is claimed.
Scoped to Aristotle's own treatises as presented in the sources; later Peripatetic, Hellenistic, and medieval Aristotelianisms are out of scope.
No claims are recorded for fundamental_problem, transformation, or destiny: Aristotle's cosmology carries no soteriological narrative in the sources used. Absence means unspecified, not denial.

claims.claim: The ultimate cause of cosmic motion is an unmoved mover: 'whatever is in motion must be moved by something else, and the first thing is not moved by something else, it is itself motionless' — a being that 'possesses nothing but actuality', an intellect that 'thinks itself' perpetually.
claims.notes: The doctrine is textually secure, but the entry notes interpretive debate about how the Physics 8 unmoved mover relates to Metaphysics 12's first mover 'moving as an object of love and striving'.

claims.claim: Natural things are compounds of matter and form: 'matter provides the potentialities which are actualised by the form'.
The cosmos and its motion are eternal, without beginning: 'motion and change in the universe can have no beginning, because the occurrence of change presupposes a previous process of change'.

Nature is 'an inner principle of change and being at rest', explanation is fourfold and teleological — 'final causes account for processes and entities by being what these processes and entities are for' — and 'nature does nothing in vain'; moreover, 'for each motion, whether natural or contrary to nature, there needs to exist a mover'.

Eternal celestial bodies govern a dependent sublunary realm of generation and corruption: 'the Sun's course along the ecliptic is responsible for many sublunar changes, the cycle of the seasons being foremost among them'.
Time is tied to eternal celestial motion: 'the motion of the eternal bodies is what makes time, so the life and being of sublunary things depends on them'.

The soul is the form of a living body, not an independent substance: 'the psyche is defined by Aristotle as the first actuality of a living animal, which is the form of a natural body potentially having life'.
Humans are distinguished within nature by rational activity: 'the human function must be an activity of the psyche not without reason'.

distinguishing_claims: The cosmos is eternal, with no creation and no end
An unmoved mover of pure actuality is the ultimate cause of motion
Explanation is teleological: natures act for ends, and 'nature does nothing in vain'
A divided cosmos: eternal celestial motions govern a sublunary realm of change
common_confusions: The unmoved mover is not a creator: the world is eternal and the mover causes motion, not existence from nothing.
Aristotelian teleology is immanent in natures, not the plan of a designing agent as in the Timaeus or classical theism.
The soul as form of the body is not the separable immortal soul of Platonism or Christianity.

sources.title: Aristotle's Natural Philosophy (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.author: Istvan Bodnar
sources.passage: Nature, according to Aristotle, is an inner principle of change and being at rest (Physics 2.1, 192b20–23). [...] final causes account for processes and entities by being what these processes and entities are for. [...] Motion and change in the universe can have no beginning, because the occurrence of change presupposes a previous process of change. [...] For each motion, whether natural or contrary to nature, there needs to exist a mover. [...] The mover of these spheres possesses nothing but actuality. [...] the Sun's course along the ecliptic is responsible for many sublunar changes, the cycle of the seasons being foremost among them. (First published 2006-05-26; substantive revision 2023-04-24.)

sources.title: Aristotle (Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
sources.passage: whatever is in motion must be moved by something else, and the first thing is not moved by something else, it is itself motionless (Met. 1073a25–34). [...] the primary being as an intellect or a kind of intellect that 'thinks itself' perpetually (Met. 1072b19–20). [...] the motion of the eternal bodies is what makes time, so the life and being of sublunary things depends on them. [...] nature does nothing in vain (GA 741b5). [...] The psyche is defined by Aristotle as the first actuality of a living animal, which is the form of a natural body potentially having life (DA 412a19–22). [...] the human function must be an activity of the psyche not without reason (EN 1097b24–1098a18). (Author byline not captured at access time — verify on review.)
